subject: ap-world-history
theme: Cold War and Decolonization, 1900–present: The Cold War bipolar order and the decolonization of Asia and Africa reshape the global political landscape, with newly independent nations navigating superpower rivalry.

core_concepts.title: Cold War Origins and Structures
core_concepts.summary: The Cold War (1947–1991) was an ideological, economic, and geopolitical rivalry between the US-led capitalist West and the Soviet-led communist East — fought through proxy wars, arms races, and ideological competition rather than direct military confrontation (prevented by mutual assured destruction through nuclear weapons). Key structures: NATO (1949, Western military alliance) vs. Warsaw Pact (1955, Eastern military alliance); Marshall Plan ($13 billion US reconstruction aid to Western Europe, 1948) to prevent communist electoral victories; Truman Doctrine (1947) — US commitment to 'contain' communism wherever threatened. Churchill's 'Iron Curtain' speech (1946) defined the ideological divide.

core_concepts.title: Cold War Conflicts and Crises
core_concepts.summary: The Cold War generated multiple armed conflicts and crises. Korean War (1950–53): US-led UN forces vs. North Korea and China, ending in an armistice and continued division. Cuban Missile Crisis (1962): 13-day nuclear standoff after US discovered Soviet missiles in Cuba, the Cold War's most dangerous moment, resolved through Soviet withdrawal and US pledges not to invade Cuba. Vietnam War (1955–75): US intervention to prevent communist North Vietnam from unifying the country; US withdrawal (1973) and eventual communist victory (1975) demonstrated limits of military power against guerrilla warfare and nationalist movements. Soviet-Afghan War (1979–89): Soviet occupation met fierce US-backed mujahideen resistance.

core_concepts.title: Decolonization: Asia
core_concepts.summary: WWII accelerated decolonization by weakening European imperial powers and strengthening colonial independence movements. India: Gandhi's nonviolent satyagraha movement — civil disobedience, boycotts, and noncooperation — built mass support for Indian independence. Britain granted independence in 1947, but partition into India (Hindu-majority) and Pakistan (Muslim-majority) triggered massive religious violence killing 200,000–2 million. Vietnam: Ho Chi Minh's Viet Minh defeated France at Dien Bien Phu (1954), but partition and US intervention followed. Indonesia: Sukarno declared independence from the Netherlands (1945). China: Communist victory under Mao Zedong (1949) created the People's Republic.

core_concepts.title: Decolonization: Africa
core_concepts.summary: Sub-Saharan Africa achieved independence primarily in the 1950s–1960s — the 'Year of Africa' (1960) saw 17 nations gain independence. Kwame Nkrumah led Ghana to independence (1957), the first sub-Saharan African nation, and promoted Pan-Africanism — the idea of African cultural and political solidarity. Some decolonization was relatively peaceful (British West Africa); others were violent. Algeria's war for independence from France (1954–1962) killed ~1 million Algerians. Apartheid South Africa (1948–1994): racial segregation system maintained by the National Party; Nelson Mandela's ANC used armed resistance (after nonviolence failed), and Mandela was imprisoned 27 years before becoming South Africa's first Black president.

core_concepts.title: The Non-Aligned Movement
core_concepts.summary: Many newly independent nations resisted choosing between US and Soviet camps through the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM). The Bandung Conference (1955, Indonesia) brought together 29 Asian and African nations, establishing principles of non-alignment, sovereignty, and anti-colonialism. Leaders: Jawaharlal Nehru (India), Gamal Abdel Nasser (Egypt), Sukarno (Indonesia), Nkrumah (Ghana), and Tito (Yugoslavia). The NAM used Cold War competition to extract aid and trade concessions from both superpowers while maintaining formal neutrality. However, 'non-alignment' was often impossible in practice as both superpowers sought to draw nations into their orbit.

core_concepts.title: End of the Cold War and Soviet Collapse
core_concepts.summary: Gorbachev's reforms (glasnost — openness; perestroika — restructuring) attempted to modernize the Soviet system without abandoning communism, but instead accelerated its collapse. The 1989 revolutions swept away communist governments in Eastern Europe without Soviet military intervention (Gorbachev's 'Sinatra Doctrine' — let each country decide its own path). The Berlin Wall fell November 9, 1989. The Soviet Union dissolved December 25, 1991, as 15 republics declared independence. Causes of Soviet collapse: economic stagnation, military overextension (Afghanistan), ideological delegitimization, and nationalist movements in Soviet republics. The US declared victory in the Cold War, leading to optimistic claims about the permanence of liberal democracy ('End of History' — Fukuyama).

key_terms.definition: Ideological and geopolitical rivalry (1947–1991) between the US-led capitalist West and Soviet-led communist East; fought through proxy wars, arms race, and ideological competition rather than direct military conflict.
US Cold War strategy (articulated by diplomat George Kennan) of preventing Soviet communism from spreading beyond its existing boundaries through economic, political, and military means.

US President Truman's declaration of US commitment to supporting free peoples resisting subjugation — used to justify military and economic aid to Greece and Turkey; became basis of containment policy.

US program providing $13 billion in reconstruction aid to Western Europe to rebuild war-devastated economies and prevent communist electoral victories — successful in stabilizing Western European democracies.
13-day nuclear standoff after US discovered Soviet missiles in Cuba; resolved through Soviet withdrawal and US pledge not to invade Cuba — the Cold War's most dangerous confrontation.
Movement of newly independent nations refusing to align with either the US or Soviet bloc in the Cold War; established at the Bandung Conference (1955).
Conference of 29 Asian and African nations in Indonesia establishing non-alignment, anti-colonialism, and sovereignty principles; foundational moment of the Global South's political self-assertion.
Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolent resistance — 'truth force' or 'soul force' — using civil disobedience, boycotts, and noncooperation to resist colonial authority and achieve Indian independence.

Division of British India into two independent nations — India (Hindu-majority) and Pakistan (Muslim-majority) — accompanied by massive population displacement and religious violence killing hundreds of thousands to 2 million.

Vietnamese communist nationalist leader who led the Viet Minh in defeating France at Dien Bien Phu (1954) and the North Vietnamese government against US intervention; icon of anti-colonial nationalism.
Ideology asserting the solidarity and common interests of all African peoples; advocated political unity and liberation from colonialism; associated with Nkrumah, Du Bois, and others.
South African system of legislated racial segregation (1948–1994) enforcing White minority supremacy over Black, Colored, and Indian populations through laws governing residence, work, and movement.
South African anti-apartheid leader of the ANC; imprisoned 27 years (1964–1990); became South Africa's first Black president (1994–1999) after negotiating the end of apartheid.
Gorbachev's reforms: glasnost (openness — reduced censorship) and perestroika (restructuring — limited market reforms) intended to modernize the Soviet system; instead accelerated its collapse.
Wall built by East Germany to prevent citizens from escaping to West Berlin; its fall on November 9, 1989 symbolized the end of the Cold War and the collapse of European communism.

exam_tip: Decolonization comparison essays are extremely common. Know the key contrast between violent and nonviolent decolonization — Gandhi's India (nonviolent but still involving enormous partition violence) vs. Algeria (prolonged armed struggle). Connect decolonization to Cold War dynamics: newly independent nations were battlegrounds for superpower competition; the Non-Aligned Movement was a strategic attempt to extract benefits from both sides. For the Cold War's end, always go beyond 'Gorbachev's reforms' — the deeper causes include Soviet economic stagnation and Afghan military overextension. Never confuse containment (Kennan's strategy) with the Truman Doctrine (its political expression).
